Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 7
Speaker 1: Hey guys, welcome to module seven and this is the final course subsequence of our
launch or funnel and also the one, where if done right, you can expect the largest
percentage of your sales to roll in. However, it's also one of the most often
misunderstood sequences and that's why if you're only going to come away with one
thing in this entire program, I want it to be this. We are not selling urgency.
Typically, at this point, you'll just see a barrage of warnings. 48 hours, 24 hours, 12
hours, 8 hours, 6 hours, 2 hours. "Oh, I swear I'm going to take it down. I'm sending you
another email right now because if I don't, you'll be angry," and there's all sorts of
desperate, sometimes cringe-worthy FOMO driven stuff. The only reason this works, if
we're getting an uptake in sales, is because while you're staying on their mind. You're
top of mind, you're relevant, you're in their inbox, there is that frequency and that just
being present and vivid and they see it.
The other one is, yes, of course, urgency and FOMO does work to a certain degree but if
they haven't bought by this point, well there may be some other stuff to coach to get
them across and any success I've had as a marketer has come not by asking why 3% of
prospects bought by asking why 97% of prospects didn't, because that's where the gold
is and that's where we're going to plant our shovels right now and start digging.
Here we go. If we're not selling urgency, then what are we selling? If you're launch or
funnel has built-in time urgency with a defined close date, then the time urgency will
already be a naturally occurring factor. You can even have a countdown timer achieving
that goal. But your job as a human, as human who has empathy, is to assess and
communicate the more subtle precursors that will make a prospect, so yes.
If we're not selling that service level urgency or at least solely relying on that urgency,
then what are we actually selling and this has been the biggest game changer for me.
We're selling decisiveness. Because when you really think about it, the reason most
people don't buy isn't because they decided not to but because they didn't decide at all.
While time urgency certain does play a role in getting people to decide by no means, the
only thing or even the most powerful thing in our job here is to not ditch service level
urgency entirely but to build upon it, to hit those precursors right below the surface that
support your prospects ability to make a decision, which in turn, will amplify our ability
to convert into closing days and hours of the sequence.
While the main thing we're coaching here is decisiveness, how do we actually coach it?
A good place to start is to counter the things that impair or get in the way of decision
making. On the service level of that, one of the main reasons people don't make
decisions is either too much information or not enough information. That's why, as we
mentioned in previous module, we're very careful not start throwing more and more
sales arguments against the wall and overwhelming them to the point of decision
paralysis.
The same goes for too many pricing or packaging options. Barry Schwartz, no relation,
has a book called "The Paradox of Choice," which really warns us as marketers not to
overwhelm the customer with options and information. The main takeaway here is to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 7 --

Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 7
really avoid building too many extra sales arguments and to not have more than two or
max, three, packages or pricing options.
What I really want to focus on here, which is in line with coaching to conversion is a little
deeper, a little more subcutaneous. It's those hidden factors that either hinder or
encourage decision making and that's what we're here to coach.
How to coach decisiveness? I like to start here, by acknowledging and removing
cognitive bias. There might be some unconscious objections that prospects might have.
Maybe they took an action or inaction in the past that they feel shame, guilt or remorse
around in buying your product would just be too confronting. Starting with the question,
what does my prospect have to forgive themselves for to really, really open themselves
up to this product? Just asking this can open up a flood of really interesting insights for
you. That's one starting point.
Another one, you've seen it earlier, it comes back again. We are coaching exclusive
empowerment. Just like how this is a crucial trigger for getting your prospect to take
that milestone action that brought them onto the journey, it also bookends the buying
journey. Just like how this principle nearly converted me into a 3-year $100,000 into a
law program I didn't even want to get into, it's an even better way to motivate people to
decide when that decision actually correlates with cashing in on their privilege points in
a way they want to, in a way that really aligns with their context and the world and
reality they want to live in.
The FOMO around leaving exclusivity points on the table can even be more powerful
than the FOMO missing out on your product or the outcome it provides. You really want
to leverage that, give people a chance to cash in on those privilege points.
The third thing we're coaching is identification with the buyer, we're continuing this. We
did in the last module. We're going to really ramp it up here and really coach this aspect
right until the end because the more we can get your prospect to identify with being a
buyer, the easier it is for them to make a decision that feels natural and aligned with
who and what they identify as.
However, here is the important note around this. We have this habit of setting the bar
of being a buyer way too high. Like we talked about in the anatomy of a buyer lesson in
module two, part of what we have to do is make the idea or makeup of a buyer more
accessible. Lower the bar in the sense that they don't need to be 1,000,000% confident.
They just have to be 100% committed to giving it an honest shot. The more specific it
can be about what that shot looks like, the better.
But instead, you know, we have this tendency to set the bar too high to be clear. One
thing that really ticks me off in this is we procrasti-shame. We procrasti-shame. We
make these claims. We say we only want to work with action takers who are 110%
committed and while it's good to hold them to that aspiration, here's the thing. The
action takers have already taken action. They bought the moment you said go. They
bought five days ago when you opened cart. By procrasti-shaming and setting the bar to
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 7 --

Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 7
only welcoming customers who are 110% confident and committed, you're, in turn,
excluding most of your potential buyers. Buyers who could be a great fit and get the
result if just given the chance and the right coaching at this stage of your funnel.
Your job isn't to raise the bar and taunt them when they can't clear it. It's to lower it to a
point where they can and then doing everything you can to help them rise into
something greater on the other side, whatever that looks like in your program or
business. In this stage, to coach decisiveness and help your prospect identify as a buyer,
we lower the bar a little bit. We make it inclusive. We say that it's okay if they have their
doubts, hesitation and resistance and that's not a barrier to entry.
All they need is a minimum viable commitment and this is how we set a bar to a level
they can clear, where we give them a chance to express courage and commitment in a
way that's accessible with where they're currently at instead of shaming or guilting
them for their lack of ironclad confidence. What we're doing is we're lowering the bar to
raise commitment and a quick example that I want to take you through this is to just ask
you what feels more inviting. "Only join 10X launches if you're 110% committed to
putting the strategies you learn into immediate excuse free action," or we get it, "You
have a rough relationship with copywriting for far too long to 1,010% believe you can
turn into the second coming of Ogilvy overnight. Yes, even with the help of two strange
Canadians who've secretly written for half of the world's online launches in the last five
years."
To that, we say, "Cool." BYOB, bring your own doubts and let Ry and I broker a peace
deal once and for all. Let us give you your login deets and if after poking around a few
lessons this weekend and firing off a few team plays, you don't get a sense of, "Holy
crap, I think I'm starting to get it," just fire us an email or text and we'll do the whole
refund dance. No farm. No foul.
Think about that for a second. The reason the second one feels better is because it
comes back down to a core principle of coaching which is empathy. No validating where
your prospect is at and making that okay, instead of creating this unrealistic caricature
of something they have trouble projecting themselves into.
This isn't to say we don't create a high standard for the type of customer we want to
work with but it does mean that we leave room for natural human behavior, which for
most of us, includes anxiety, uncertainty and skepticism. You should look at your own
buying experience for this. Are you 110% sure all the time? Do you ever experience
anxiety, hesitation or resistance and if not, if you actually do experience these things,
why are you setting the bar so unrealistically high for your audience? No one is 100%
confident about anything. No one's 100% confident they'll be alive tomorrow, that the
person they love will be around forever, so why should they be 100% confident in what
you're selling and their ability to make the most of it.
At the end of the day, the best way to overcome is to include. We take the path of least
resistance by offering your audience the minimum viable commitment while deeply
acknowledging and reflecting back exactly how they're feeling in that moment and
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 7 --

Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 4 of 7
making that feeling congruent with the buyer experience and in doing so, give them the
most frictionless path beyond the buying line.
Here is what I like to reference as a buyer. They're allowed to bring doubt, skepticism,
fear and resistance but just as long as confidence, trust and excitement outweighs it,
then you're going to get the sale.
Now, the flip side of the minimum viable commitment is the quick and valuable win.
This is where you really need to think about the ownership experience of your product
and how this ties into how you design your course itself. But for the purposes of
marketing your program, it's super important to identify, what not only the quickest win
would be but the quickest and most valuable win. Know what element of your program
can your prospect dive into, literally hours within getting their login or access info and
generate an important outcome or solve one of the most painful elements of the
problem. It may not be the final culmination of your program but it's such that even if
that's all they got after minutes or hours of using it, it would make it well worth the
while.
Quick wins is a concept I've heard before from Stanford researcher, BJ Fogg and Danny
Iny from Mirasee, as it relates to online course creation but what this is is it's essentially
used to enhance user experience and build momentum in the early stages of your
course. But to take this beyond instructional design and actually leverage the concept,
to coach decisiveness and increase conversions, the quick and valuable win is something
we need to take a deeper look at. The quick and valuable win has real world, tangible
value. It's worth something and it's something that they could achieve within minutes.
There's an ROI there.
It addresses an aspect of the moment of highest tension. It's a very quick acting pain
reliever. There's that immediate pain relief or pleasure provider depending on what
you're doing and there's that instant gratification or relief. This isn't just a quick crunchy
win that they can achieve within minutes of receiving your product but it also has
tangible, real world value to it. It addresses some aspect of their moment of highest
tension. It may not produce the ultimate and final outcome but it acts as a much needed
hit of morphine for your prospects most urgent problem or a highly practical and
immediate win that's an important piece to the final outcome.
This is important. That is Mount Everest and just like how, I mean, anyone would be
crazy to climb it but you'd be more likely to take that 10-week perilous journey up
Everest if you knew that on day 2, you'd hit a majestic ridge that legend holds, possesses
an elixir of youth and vitality. Sure, the two-week journey is important to you and
aspirational and might have this lifelong goal and outcome that you've been trying to
achieve, but you'd be a lot more likely to go on that journey if there was a valuable and
quick and tangible reward on day two or a few days in.
This is also congruent with what world class performance coach, Todd Herman, says
about our horizon line for seeing outcomes. While your course or program may promise
these amazing and meaningful outcomes in three to four months from now, the reality
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 7 --

Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 5 of 7
of that outcome is just too far off in the distance for your prospect to reel in and identify
with and ground in their reality. It's important from a marketing and decisiveness
perspective to reel that horizon line and ownership experience to the next few hours or
days. The further out your future pace, the more blurry that is and the more blurry it is,
the less real and vivid it is in their life.
You really want to focus on this quick win that's also valuable, the highest possible ROI
that you can deliver. Then you approach and paradigm towards writing copy which we
call coaching the conversion, we also offer that immediate quick, valuable, win of diving
into whatever phase of your launch you need the most help with. Most likely, it's going
to be in the sales and closing sequences in applying one of templates included. This is
something, which can and has generated 2X, 5X or even 10X ROI in literally a few hours
providing you're the right fit for the program and exclusively empowered to get those
results.
Another example would be a meditation program for entrepreneurs. If you're selling a
mindfulness or meditation program to frantic entrepreneurs who have trouble
disconnecting and live with this constant tension and anxiety, sure, over the course of 4,
6, 12 weeks, you'll ground them in a totally new way of experiencing life. A flipped
paradigm where they'll be able to relate to situations with more clarity of mind,
peacefulness and resourcefulness, but if their moment of highest tension, their bleeding
moment is that they're constantly afraid of failure or messing up and they have this
increasing anxiety that stops them from being present with their family, then maybe
their quick valuable win here is a bank of special, short, guided meditations that promise
to dissolve and relax that within 10 minutes. That immediate painkiller and that's the
QVW.
In the workbook in the next section, we're going to walk you through that. How to
uncover that, how to actually mark down what that is for your program and to offer that
and to really leverage that in your closing sequence.
The next thing we're coaching here is binary choice. To coach decisiveness, we need to
make sure that our prospect is aware that a binary choice exists. That a choice does
indeed exist and must be made. This is arguably, the biggest service level reason why
personal selling with follow-ups closes 10X higher than online strategies because it
requires decision. They can't just remove themselves from the context without making a
clear and conscious decision that yes, they want to move forward or no, not for me.
Versus the online world where they ca choose not to choose. Let the deadline pass them
by and then smooth their FOMO wounds with rationalizations around why it wasn't a
good fit later. While we can't force a decision via online marketing, we can definitely
encourage and incentivize one.
Now, from a marketing perspective, it's far more powerful to have someone take a
negative action like saying no than doing nothing at all. No gives us insight. Especially if
we ask why. No allows for the continuation of a conversation but silence doesn't. In the
workbook, you'll find a few templates that addresses head on and coach people into
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 7 --

Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 6 of 7
knowing and realizing that there is indeed a choice to be made and responsibility to
themselves to make one.
Which leads to the next thing we're coaching which is control, collaboration and co-
creation because our prospect will be more likely to acknowledge choice and their
responsibility to choose if they feel a sense of control around it. We resist when we feel
pushed. It's not a matter of telling them they must choose now, but instead, giving them
the conditions in which they feel a sense of power to do it. This works especially well if
they've had a hand in the thing they're choosing. This is where co-creation comes into
play.
For example, if you take their pulse 24 hours before the close and ask them, "What do
you need to make a decision or what's stopping you?" And then slightly tailor your close
to address that missing link. They'll feel less like your pushing an offer to them and more
like their collaborating with you to make it work. Now, of course this isn't the easiest
thing to do at scale but in the work book you'll see a template that works to leverage
this principle, the essence of it and empower your prospect to co-create a close and in
doing so, feel more empowered to take ownership over the decision.
Then, here's a big one, second hand FOMO. This is another tool I use to coach
decisiveness. Making the decision more interpersonal. It's no longer just their FOMO, it's
our fear of them missing out. How their decision or lack thereof can affect other
stakeholders. This is important. This is really big when we could share our own fear of
them missing out. Reminding them of why you do what you do, what breaks your heart
and how can they be part of helping that. You'll see a template later as well, that will
really dive in deeper. How their decision affects other objects and things and how it's
not just about them.
The point here is to show them that the consequences of indecisiveness goes beyond
their own concern and it gives them a chance to be more responsible towards those
other factors. When we stop making the decision all about them, we actually start
channeling their empathy to feel that their decision is part of a bigger whole, which can
really encourage conscious decision making. Those are really the precursors, the stuff
beneath the iceberg that we're really going to be working on and coaching through the
final 48 hours of your launch but before into the workbook to tackle this part that's a
crucial part of your sequence here, here is another final really big one.
It is the risk reversal. It's the safety in the outcomes and your ability to deliver it. In the
sales sequence, we introduce the risk revealer where we brought to light how the cost
of not moving forward is greater than moving forward. Here, we need to bring total
safety to that choice. Safety in the outcomes and your ability to deliver it and when you
do this, the combination of the risk revealer and risk reversal, that becomes risk
mitigation. The culmination of revealing the risk and then reversing it frames the buying
decision as one that mitigates overall risk. That's ultimately what makes people feel
good about making the choice. So yes, we use service level urgency and time urgency
but we also get them deeply attuned to the real urgency that comes through both
revealing risk and framing the decision as a way to mitigate it.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 7 --

Mod 7 -Main Lesson (1) (Completed 05/08/18)

Page 7 of 7
Which kind of lends itself to a technique I call pain anchoring. It's like price anchoring.
We've heard that term before where one option becomes exponentially more desirable
when compared with the other and I'll show you a little later how to anchor the pain
around indecision versus the pain and missing our completely. This could be really
powerful. We're going to explore some templates to utilize it but I want to stop this
now.
I know we've covered a lot and it's quite a bit for just this small, little 48-hour window
but since this is where the bulk of your sales are going to come, if you have an
Evergreen funnel and plugin emails that support these principles, that is going to be
automated revenue for months or years to come. That's really important that even
though this is a small sequence, we really address the true factors that make people
decide. If decisiveness is the main thing, that separates one on one selling with follow-
ups, that converts 10 times higher by just getting our prospects to a place where they
could decide, make it a very clear binary choice and giving them the tools and coaching
them around all the things that enhance their ability to do so, this is going to be a game
changer.
I'm going to leave you here. I'm going to meet you back in the workbook where we'll
dive into some questions to consider and then we'll dive even deeper into series of
templates that will activate and leverage all these principles we just discussed and really
take the guesswork out of it. That's it. I really, really appreciate you being here and
sticking through all these concepts. Watch it again if you have to but if you're ready, I
will meet you in the workbook. I'll see you there.
